items.q: Does repeating a piece over and over guarantee improvement?
items.correct: No - improvement comes from correct, focused repetition: isolating problems, slowing down and fixing errors before speeding up.
items.misconception: Practice makes perfect, so any repetition automatically helps.
items.random_wrong: You improve most by buying a more expensive instrument.
items.why: Repeating mistakes ingrains them; 'perfect practice makes perfect' captures the need for accurate, deliberate repetition rather than mileage alone.

items.q: Will choosing a harder piece always earn a higher mark?
items.correct: No - an accurate, musical performance of a suitable piece scores better than a struggling attempt at one beyond your technique.
items.misconception: Difficulty alone impresses, so always pick the hardest piece.
items.random_wrong: Marks depend mainly on the length of the piece's title.
items.why: Assessment rewards the quality of the outcome; difficulty only helps when it is delivered with control, so realistic repertoire choice is itself a skill.

items.q: Can dynamics and expression be added at the last minute?
items.correct: It is far better to build them in from early practice so they become part of how you know the piece.
items.misconception: Learn the notes first; expression can be sprinkled on the night before.
items.random_wrong: Expression is only required in vocal performances.
items.why: Habits formed in practice surface under pressure; performers who rehearse expressively perform expressively, while bolt-on dynamics usually vanish in the exam.

items.q: What makes a good choice of performance piece?
items.correct: One that suits your level and shows your strengths
items.misconception: The hardest piece you can find
items.why: It's One that suits your level and shows your strengths. “The hardest piece you can find” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Keeping going with a steady pulse
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Keeping going with a steady pulse. “Playing everything very fast” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the best way to improve a difficult bar?
items.correct: Isolate it, slow it down and repeat it correctly
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Isolate it, slow it down and repeat it correctly. “Play the whole piece faster” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why is recording your own practice useful?
items.correct: You hear yourself objectively and can target improvements
items.misconception: It replaces the need to practise
items.why: Correct answer: You hear yourself objectively and can target improvements. “It replaces the need to practise” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
